sourceTitle: The ultimate quickstart guide to Notion for Enterprise

questions.question: Which user type in Notion Enterprise has the most limited access and cannot have a personal section in the workspace?

options.text: Member
options.explanation: Members have full access to the workspace and a personal section.

options.text: Guest
options.explanation: Guests are free users limited to pages they’re granted and have no personal section.

options.text: Workspace owner
options.explanation: Workspace owners have full privileges, including a personal section.

options.text: Membership admin
options.explanation: Membership admins are an administrative role with broader permissions.

questions.question: If an organization wants to prevent users from creating new teamspaces, which setting should they adjust?

options.text: Toggle "Limit teamspace creation" under Settings > Teamspaces
options.explanation: Enabling this toggle restricts teamspace creation to workspace owners only.

options.text: Disable "Default teamspaces" in the Workspace Settings
options.explanation: This only changes which teamspaces members join automatically, not creation rights.

options.text: Turn off "Allow guests" in the teamspace settings
options.explanation: That setting only affects guest access, not who can create teamspaces.

options.text: Remove the "Teamspaces" option from the sidebar
options.explanation: The sidebar cannot be edited to remove the option; creation rights are controlled by the toggle.

questions.question: Why are public email domains like gmail.com not allowed to be added as Allowed Email Domains?

options.text: Because they are considered insecure and could expose the workspace to anyone with a Gmail address.
options.explanation: Public domains are disallowed to prevent uncontrolled access; only private, organization‑owned domains can be allowed.

options.text: Because Notion only supports corporate domains for billing purposes.
options.explanation: Billing is separate; the restriction is about access control, not billing.

options.text: Because Gmail accounts cannot be verified via DNS TXT records.
options.explanation: Verification is possible, but the policy blocks public domains regardless of verification.

options.text: Because Notion automatically blocks any domain that is not listed in the SCIM configuration.
options.explanation: SCIM is unrelated to the Allowed Email Domains feature.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a step required to enable SAML SSO for a Notion workspace?

options.text: Verify your email domain by adding a TXT record to DNS
options.explanation: Domain verification is required before enabling SAML SSO.

options.text: Enable SAML SSO in the Identity & provisioning settings
options.explanation: Enabling SSO is a core step.

options.text: Change the default login method to "Only SAML SSO"
options.explanation: Setting the default login enforces SSO for users.

options.text: Create a new workspace for each user to use SAML SSO
options.explanation: Creating a separate workspace per user is unnecessary and not part of the SSO setup process.

questions.question: When would you recommend disabling Just‑in‑Time (JIT) provisioning?

options.text: When you are also using SCIM to provision users
options.explanation: Enabling both JIT and SCIM can cause mismatched membership states, so it’s advised to disable JIT when SCIM is active.

options.text: When you want users to be able to join via an invite link
options.explanation: Invite links are independent of JIT provisioning.

options.text: When you have no Identity Provider configured
options.explanation: JIT works with SAML SSO; lack of an IdP means SSO isn’t configured at all.

options.text: When you only allow Guest users in the workspace
options.explanation: Guests are not provisioned via JIT; the recommendation is unrelated.

questions.question: Which action can a Workspace owner take from the Domain Management view for a single‑member workspace?

options.text: Require the account owner to change to a non‑corporate email address
options.explanation: The "Require account ownership change" action forces a non‑corporate email before access is restored.

options.text: Upgrade the workspace directly to the Enterprise plan
options.explanation: Single‑member workspaces cannot be directly upgraded; they must be claimed and then upgraded.

options.text: Add a new Membership admin to the workspace
options.explanation: Membership admins are added via the Members settings, not from Domain Management.

options.text: Enable SAML SSO for that workspace
options.explanation: SAML SSO is configured in Identity & provisioning, not from the Domain Management view.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Audit Log in Notion Enterprise?

options.text: To provide a searchable history of events and actions across the workspace
options.explanation: The Audit Log records a wide range of events, giving administrators visibility into activity.

options.text: To automatically back up all pages in the workspace
options.explanation: Backing up pages is handled by export features, not the Audit Log.

options.text: To manage billing and subscription changes
options.explanation: Billing is managed in the Billing settings, not the Audit Log.

options.text: To control which integrations can be installed
options.explanation: Integration permissions are set in the Connections settings.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Guest users is correct?

options.text: Guests can be added at the workspace level via the Members page.
options.explanation: Guests are added per page through the Share menu, not at the workspace level.

options.text: Guests are supported by SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning.
options.explanation: Guests are explicitly not supported by SAML SSO or SCIM.

options.text: Guests have access only to the specific pages they are invited to.
options.explanation: Guests are limited to the pages they’re granted access to and have no personal workspace.

options.text: Guests can create new teamspaces for their projects.
options.explanation: Only workspace owners (or those with permission) can create teamspaces; guests cannot.

questions.question: What are two main benefits of consolidating multiple workspaces into a single primary workspace?

options.text: Simplified admin controls and more discoverable content
options.explanation: Consolidation centralizes admin tasks and makes content easier to find and collaborate on.

options.text: Reduced billing costs and automatic SAML SSO configuration
options.explanation: While billing may be affected, consolidation does not automatically configure SAML SSO.

options.text: Elimination of all guest users and removal of all integrations
options.explanation: Guests and integrations can still exist after consolidation; they are not automatically removed.

options.text: Ability to claim workspaces without upgrading to Enterprise plan
options.explanation: Claiming workspaces upgrades them to Enterprise; consolidation does not bypass that requirement.

questions.question: If an organization wants to limit who can join external (non‑organization) workspaces, which setting should they modify?

options.text: External workspace access under User Management
options.explanation: This setting controls whether managed users can join workspaces outside the organization.

options.text: Allow users to change account information
options.explanation: That setting relates to profile edits, not external workspace access.

options.text: Session Duration
options.explanation: Session duration determines how long a login lasts, not workspace access.

options.text: Disable public page sharing
options.explanation: Public page sharing affects page visibility, not workspace joining permissions.
